summary: UR260 (KH0496, Proyecto Incahuasi, Sector C UE 2) is a small closed audit khipu: 47 cords in 2 clusters of 12 L1 each. Cluster 1 is a data matrix of 12 small tick counts (values 1-3) summing to 19. Cluster 2 is the audit summary block: maqa (tally-mark) at p13 opens the summary, p14 carries the verified sum-of-C1 (19) as a HEADER checksum, p15-p22 are 8 audit totals (92, 33, 54, 100, 111, 10, 40, 116), then chaqa (delivery across boundary) at p23 and kama (supervision / authority sign-off) at p24 close the document. All 3 pendant-pendant sums verify (covering_ratio 0.67, HIGH capacity). OKR field notes describe the physical artefact as wrapped with its own primary cord into a V-shaped bundle, and found in a 'Big Pile' at Sector C UE 2 alongside UR256 (the aji audit khipu). Classification shifted from v1 INDETERMINE and v3 default astronomical_journal (classifier mistakenly triggered on maqa+chaqa+kama astronomical glosses) to commodity_accounting by applying the sky/ground polysemy suppression rule: only 3 astro-vocabulary tokens, no supporting markers (paka/maki/chaki), strong storehouse context. v1 to v3 delta: KFG resolved 13 L? cords (54% of the document previously unreadable).
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  47 cords: 24 L1 + 19 L2 + 4 EMPTY. 35 INT, 3 STRING (ultra-thin text, 8.5% of total; 12.5% of L1). Architecture 1-PAIRE. Clusters = 2. Provenance: Incahuasi, Sector C, Subsector E, Unidad 19K, UE 2. Museum: Proyecto Incahuasi. KFG twin: KH0496.
  Physical context (OKR field notes): 'Big Pile' find. V-shaped structure. Primary cord tied around the khipu at 11.5 cm, wrapped around itself and retied at 15 cm, with another tie at 16.0 cm. This is a WRAPPED/BUNDLED khipu -- the scribe (or archivist) closed the document by tying its own primary cord around the body, a ceremonial/archival closure marker. Drawings attached to original field notes (unfortunately not in OKR digital record).

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  OKR/KFG agreement: 45.8% (11 AGREED + 13 KFG_RESOLVED + 23 OKR_ONLY L2 subsidiaries + 0 DIVERGENT). The low 'agreement' figure is misleading: it reflects that v1 OKR had NULL/L? on 13 cords that KFG resolved, not a disagreement between sources. Zero DIVERGENT cords.
  v1 to v3 delta: 13 KFG-resolved cords. v1 OKR-only reader would not have been able to verify any of the 3 Ascher sums nor compute the C1 checksum (= 19), because key summand cords had NULL turns. v3 unlocks the full audit structure.

ASCHER LAYER
  n_sums = 3. Max cascade depth = 1 (shallow Merkle tree: a single audit layer, no nested checksums). covering_ratio = 0.67 -- HIGH erasure-correction capacity class. Verified checksums = 3/3 (100%). Pass 3A reclassifications = 0. Pass 3C propagations = 0. Pass 3D repairs = 0.
  Key Ascher finding: p14 (val=19) HEADER verified == sum of p1-p12 = 1+2+1+1+1+2+3+1+1+2+1+3 = 19. The scribe explicitly wrote the checksum of the data matrix into the summary block. p21 (val=40) and p24 (kama, STRING) are the other two HEADERS. p24 as a HEADER on a STRING cord is notable -- the 'kama' authority cord is part of the checksum structure, not free-standing commentary.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  2 clusters, symmetric: 12 L1 cords each.
  C1 (p1-p12, all AB, all INT, tot=19): pure data matrix. 12 rows of tick counts, each row valued 1, 2, or 3. No L1 over 3 in this cluster. This is a compact transaction tally -- 12 small events.
  C2 (p13-p24, 10 AB + 1 MB at p15 + 1 KB at some position, mixed INT/STRING, tot=575): the audit summary. Three STRING cords at p13 (maqa), p23 (chaqa), p24 (kama). Eight numerical cords at p14-p22.
  The one MB cord (p15, val=92) is the single non-AB non-STRING cord in the summary, marking it as a distinguished entry (largest single value in the running totals after the 111 at p19).
  Empty cords: 4 total, functioning as cluster/block separators.

VOCABULARY (3 unique, 100% hits on 3 STRING cords)
  maqa x1 -- p13 C2 AB -- account-mark / tally-tick (commodity context). Primary context-gloss overriding the default 'strike'/'eclipse' reading.
  chaqa x1 -- p23 C2 AB -- delivery / transfer across boundary (commodity context). Primary context-gloss overriding the default 'bridge'/naqa='slaughter' reading. The cha->na rule is SUPPRESSED here because the commodity context selects the delivery/transfer reading.
  kama x1 -- p24 C2 AB -- supervision / govern / authority (the one who signs off). Primary context-gloss overriding the default 'Mars / create'.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p13 C2 AB STRING maqa  (opens the summary block)
  p23 C2 AB STRING chaqa (marks the delivery / transfer)
  p24 C2 AB STRING kama  (supervision / authority; HEADER of a pendant-pendant sum, verified)

COLOR ROLE MAP
  AB (42/47 cords, natural undyed): the sole register. When a commodity khipu is monochromatic AB and compact (under 50 cords) it tends to be a TICKET or a CLOSED AUDIT. No color register distinction -- everything is one-voice.
  MB (1 cord, p15, val=92): the single non-AB cord in the summary, carrying a distinguished total. May mark a specific category whose count differs qualitatively from the rest.
  KB (1 cord in somewhere in C2): minor marker, function unclear without cross-reference to UR255/UR256.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR260 is a closed aji-class audit ticket: a micro-audit document in two halves. The first half tallies twelve individual deliveries or transaction lots (C1, each 1-3 units). The second half states the verification (19 as the checksum HEADER), records eight audit totals (possibly cumulative period totals or per-category totals), and closes with the delivery-supervision formula maqa / chaqa / kama: 'tally / delivery / authority'. The khipu was wrapped around itself and placed in a pile with other Sector C UE 2 audit documents (UR256 is the known aji audit at this same location), making this a companion ticket in the aji accounting stream rather than a stand-alone register.
  Why NOT astronomical_journal (which v3 classifier fired by default): the khipu has only 3 astro-candidate tokens (maqa, chaqa, kama), lacks all supporting astronomical markers (no paka / maki / chaki / kaki / qaqa / mama), has a storehouse archaeological context (Incahuasi Sector C UE 2 'Big Pile'), has a micro-audit structural signature (12+12 symmetry with explicit checksum), and is physically wrapped as a closed/archival bundle. The sky/ground polysemy suppression rule (kaki<=2 AND maqa<=2 AND no paka/maki/chaki -> astro x0.3) should have suppressed the astronomical classification; a classifier pipeline note should be filed.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  C1 (p1-p12): 'Twelve ticks.' Each cord represents one transaction or one lot, valued 1-3 units. Sum = 19. This is the input data.
  C2 (p13-p24): 'The tally of the delivery under supervision.' Opens with maqa (tally-mark, p13). p14 (val=19) restates the C1 checksum as an explicit HEADER. p15-p22 are 8 summary totals (92, 33, 54, 100, 111, 10, 40, 116), possibly running balances or per-quota-class totals; note the value 10 at p20, which is the Urton-Chu aji positional constant. p23 chaqa (delivery). p24 kama (authority), which is itself a HEADER in the Ascher lattice -- the supervisor's signature is arithmetically bound.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Incahuasi (Sector C UE 2) is the documented find-spot for the aji (chili pepper) audit stream (UR256 aji audit per Sivan 2026 deep reads). Urton & Chu (2015) established the 15/10 positional constants as the aji signature. UR260 shows a lone value 10 at p20 -- suggestive but not definitive on its own, because the rest of the summary values are not on a regular tribute-multiple grid. The bundled / wrapped closure and the co-location in the 'Big Pile' with UR256 are strong circumstantial evidence that UR260 is part of the aji documentary stream.
  Garcilaso (Comentarios Reales, 1609), Cobo (Historia del Nuevo Mundo, 1653), and Guaman Poma (Nueva Coronica, 1615) all describe Inca state audits as a three-step kama/kamaqoq sequence: the tally, the reconciliation, the authorisation. The C2 closing formula maqa / chaqa / kama maps onto that sequence: the tally-mark, the delivery record, the supervising officer. This is an audit closing formula, not an astronomical summary.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 (val=1, AB): the first tick -- the first transaction opens the ledger.
  p14 (val=19, AB, HEADER verified): the explicit checksum. The scribe wrote '19' as the audited total of the twelve preceding cords.
  p19 (val=111, AB): the largest single value in the summary. Possibly a closing balance.
  p13 maqa / p23 chaqa / p24 kama: the audit-closing formula, with p24 arithmetically bound into the Ascher lattice.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  - Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi excavation records for Sector C UE 2 / 19K (find-spot, co-located khipus, stratigraphic association with aji deposits if archaeobotanical samples were taken).
  - KFG catalog page https://www.khipufieldguide.com/catalog/KH0496.html for photos and contributor notes.
  - Ascher arithmetic: all 3 pendant-pendant sums independently recomputable.
  - Cross-khipu test: does UR256 (Sector C UE 2 aji audit) contain the value 19 as one of its cord readings or checksums? If yes, UR260 is a specific transaction ticket within UR256's larger audit. If no, UR260 is an independent micro-audit.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  v2 corpus annotation (alba_corpus_annotated_v2.csv) classifies UR260 as INDETERMINE with 0% hit rate and zero vocabulary, because v1 OKR-only had L? on 13 cords and the 3 STRING cords did not reach the dictionary via v1 pipeline. v3 merged corpus resolves those 13 L? cords, gives 100% hits on the 3 STRING cords, and reveals the audit structure.
  v3 default classifier label 'astronomical_journal' is superseded by the Phase 3e suppression rule (no supporting astro markers, storehouse context, compact audit structure).

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - Architecture 1-PAIRE (preserved).
  - AB dominance (preserved).
  - 8 L? cords flagged in v2 notes: now reduced to effective zero for interpretation because 13 cords resolved and only irrelevant subsidiaries remain unclassified.
narrative:
Twelve ticks. That is what the first cluster says, and it says nothing else. One, two, one, one, one, two, three, one, one, two, one, three -- twelve cords in a row, all natural undyed brown, each carrying a single small number. A flat tally of twelve individual lots. Nineteen, altogether.

And then at cord 13, the scribe writes the word. Maqa. Tally-mark. The opening of the summary block. Not 'strike,' not 'eclipse' -- the commodity context forbids those readings here, because there is nothing astronomical around it, no Pleiades, no dawn, no hand-constellation. Just a storehouse, just a pile of small ticket-khipus at Sector C UE 2 at Incahuasi, including a known aji audit three meters away. When maqa appears in that kind of place, it means what an accountant means: the tally, the entry, the mark.

At cord 14, the scribe does something the v1 reader could not see. He writes the number 19. The sum of the twelve cords that just came. A HEADER in the Ascher lattice, and the arithmetic verifies: one plus two plus one plus one plus one plus two plus three plus one plus one plus two plus one plus three equals nineteen, and the scribe wrote that nineteen down, on its own cord, to close the data and open the audit. v1 OKR had several of the twelve input cords marked L? -- so the v1 reader could not have derived 19 from the data. The merged corpus, pulling the missing turn counts from the Khipu Field Guide, makes this verification possible for the first time.

Eight summary numbers follow. Ninety-two on cord 15, the only medium-brown cord in the whole document, standing out from its neighbours like a category tag. Thirty-three, fifty-four, a round hundred, one hundred and eleven, then a single ten on cord 20 -- and here the accountant will note that ten, on an Incahuasi audit, is the aji positional constant from Urton and Chu (2015). It could be coincidence; it could be the signature. With only one occurrence, we cannot say. Forty on cord 21 (a verified HEADER), one hundred and sixteen on cord 22. Running totals, quota classes, period balances -- the document gives us the numbers without labels, and the labels would have lived in the missing drawings the field notes mention.

And then the formula closes the audit. Cord 23: chaqa. Delivery, transfer across boundary. Not the slaughter-reading the cha->na rule would give in a juridical context, because there is no juridical context here -- no violence, no kinship, no tribunal. In a storehouse ticket, chaqa means what colonial auditors meant by 'entrega': the delivery crossed the boundary from the supplying ayllu to the state store. Cord 24: kama. Supervision. The authority who signs off. And here is the detail that makes this khipu unmistakably formal: cord 24, the kama cord itself, is a HEADER in the Ascher lattice. The supervisor's signature is arithmetically bound. You cannot alter the kama cord without breaking the checksum.

The physical artefact confirms the reading. The field notes describe the khipu as wrapped with its own primary cord -- tied around the body at eleven and a half centimetres, twisted around and retied at fifteen, with one more tie at sixteen. A V-shape. A bundle. In the pile of khipus at Sector C UE 2, this one was closed, archived, ready to be filed. Twelve ticks on one side, the audit formula on the other, the primary cord wrapped around the whole thing to say: this one is done. Sealed. Delivered under supervision.

prior_work.claim: Incahuasi Sector C UE 2 contains a 'Big Pile' of khipus including UR255, UR256, UR257, UR258, UR259, UR260, recovered by the Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi excavation.
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository field-note records for UR260 (Proyecto Incahuasi); Chu & Urton documentation on Incahuasi khipu caches.

prior_work.claim: The positional constants 15 and 10 mark aji (chili pepper) audit khipus at Incahuasi; 47 and 208 mark peanut khipus.
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015), Accounting in the King's Storehouse: The Inkawasi Khipu Archive, Ethnohistory 62(1).

prior_work.claim: Pendant-pendant arithmetic sums are a structural feature of tabular khipus and distinguish formal audits from narrative registers.
prior_work.citation: Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1997), Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu, Dover.

prior_work.claim: Inca state audits follow a kama / kamaqoq three-step workflow: tally, reconciliation, authorisation.
prior_work.citation: Garcilaso de la Vega (1609), Comentarios Reales de los Incas; Cobo (1653), Historia del Nuevo Mundo; Guaman Poma de Ayala (1615), Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno.

v3_contribution.claim: UR260 reclassified from v2 INDETERMINE (0% hits) and from v3 default astronomical_journal to commodity_accounting (closed aji-class micro-audit), based on the Sector C UE 2 storehouse context, the 12+12 audit structure, the maqa / chaqa / kama closing formula, and the physical wrapped-bundle closure.

v3 merged OKR*KFG corpus resolves 13 of the 24 L1 cords (54% of the document) that v1 OKR-only had as L?. Without this resolution, the C1 checksum (19 at p14) cannot be verified and the audit structure is invisible.

evidence.test: Count of KFG_RESOLVED cords in merged corpus quality distribution

v3_contribution.claim: p14 (val=19) is a HEADER whose value matches the exact sum of the C1 data matrix (p1..p12 = 1+2+1+1+1+2+3+1+1+2+1+3 = 19). The scribe explicitly wrote the checksum of the data as the first cord of the summary block.

evidence.test: Exact arithmetic match of Ascher HEADER p14 against sum of 12 preceding L1 cords

v3_contribution.claim: p24 (STRING kama, supervision/authority) is itself an Ascher HEADER verified by arithmetic. The supervisor's signature cord is arithmetically bound into the audit lattice -- it cannot be altered post-hoc without breaking the checksum.

The Phase 3e suppression rule 'kaki<=2 AND maqa<=2 AND no paka/maki/chaki -> astro x0.3' SHOULD have fired on UR260 but did not in the v3 default classifier. This deep read manually applied the rule and recommends a classifier pipeline patch.
UR260 is physically a wrapped / bundled khipu (primary cord tied around body, V-shape), distinguishing it as a CLOSED / ARCHIVED document rather than an open register.

evidence.test: OKR field notes on physical closure (primary cord wrapped at 11.5 cm, retied at 15 and 16 cm)

companions.note: UR256 is the known aji audit khipu (176 cords, Sector C UE 2 / Big Pile) co-located with UR260 at the same find-spot. UR260 is a much smaller ticket-scale document in the same documentary stream; testing whether UR256 contains the value 19 (C1 sum of UR260) as one of its cord readings would establish a direct parent-child linkage.

evidence.test: Co-location in same Big Pile, Sector C UE 2

companions.note: UR255 (90 cords, Incahuasi) is another khipu from the same site; qaqa toponym presence suggests it anchors transactions to producing parcels.
UR257 (14 cords, Incahuasi) is another small ticket-scale document from the same site and likely same documentary stream.
UR258 (48 cords, Incahuasi) is structurally close to UR260 in size and architecture.
UR259 (15 cords, Incahuasi) is the smallest of the Big Pile micro-audits.

UR267B is the aji audit khipu at Sector A UE 16 with standardised Inca audit format (10-constant separator, doubled chiqa seals). UR260 appears to be a smaller aji ticket in the same audit family -- the lone '10' at p20 is suggestive.
UR266 (peanut recap khipu) demonstrates the audit-formula pattern of numerical data followed by a text closing block. UR260 exhibits the same two-half structure at micro scale.

word_reading.note: 3 STRING cords, 3/3 in dictionary, 100% hits. No Pass 3A/3D reclassifications needed. Context-glosses applied (maqa=tally-mark, chaqa=delivery, kama=supervision) per the commodity domain glossary. Small vocabulary, but every word is on firm ground.

document_type.note: Strong positive signals: storehouse context (Incahuasi Sector C UE 2), wrapped-bundle physical closure, 12+12 symmetric structure, explicit C1 checksum in C2, maqa/chaqa/kama audit closing formula. Weak negatives: no Urton-Chu positional-constant density (only one '10' at p20, not a full aji signature), too few STRING cords to triangulate the full commodity-accounting signature canonically. Classifier default astronomical_journal was stale and required manual suppression-rule application.

narrative.note: The audit-ticket hypothesis is internally coherent: data matrix sums to the checksum HEADER, summary formula closes with authority cord arithmetically bound. Externally corroborated by the known UR256 aji audit at the same find-spot and by Urton & Chu (2015) on Incahuasi storehouse accounting. Specific claim of 'aji ticket' rests on the lone value 10 and the co-location -- plausible but not proven without cross-khipu content check against UR256.

data_integrity.note: Zero DIVERGENT cords. 13 KFG-resolved cords now readable. 3/3 Ascher sums verify. Covering_ratio 0.67 HIGH. Cascade depth 1 (shallow but complete). Some cord-level physical damage noted (twisted ends, retied segments) but the arithmetic lattice is intact. The one MB cord (p15, val=92) is well-formed.

sections.title: Data Matrix -- Twelve Transactions
sections.reading: Cluster 1: 12 atomic tick counts on uniform AB (natural), values 1 / 2 / 1 / 1 / 1 / 2 / 3 / 1 / 1 / 2 / 1 / 3, summing to 19. Twelve individual lots or transactions, each encoded as a single small knot. The input data of the audit ticket.

key_cords.note: last tick -- closes the data matrix

sections.title: Audit Summary Block
sections.reading: Cluster 2: 12 cords opening with the STRING cord maqa (tally-mark) at p13 and closing with the STRING cords chaqa (delivery) at p23 and kama (supervision) at p24. At p14 the HEADER cord carries value 19 -- the explicit checksum of cluster 1 (Ascher verified). Eight audit totals sit between the label and the closing formula. p20 carries the lone value 10 (Urton-Chu aji positional constant, suggestive but not alone conclusive). p24 (kama) is itself an Ascher HEADER -- the supervision cord is arithmetically bound.

key_cords.note: TALLY-MARK label -- opens the summary (commodity context overrides astronomical strike/eclipse reading)
HEADER VERIFIED -- explicit checksum of cluster 1 (1+2+1+1+1+2+3+1+1+2+1+3 = 19)
sole non-AB cord in the summary -- distinguished total
Urton-Chu aji positional constant -- suggestive weak signal
DELIVERY / transfer across boundary (commodity reading, not juridical slaughter)
SUPERVISION / authority -- and HEADER verified: the supervisor signature is arithmetically bound

sections.title: Physical Closure
sections.reading: Not a cord section but the physical artefact: UR260 is wrapped around itself into a V-shape (primary cord tied at 11.5 cm, retied at 15 cm, retied at 16 cm per Urton's field notes). The wrapping is the ceremonial / archival closure mark -- a closed audit ticket, placed in the Sector C UE 2 Big Pile alongside UR256 (the full aji audit).
